Authentication System

Overview Tech Stack Architecture Step by Step Build Key Concepts Learned Problems & Solutions Future Improvements
All Documentation Create an Account Back to Portfolio
Project Documentation

Authentication System

A full walkthrough of how the site wide user authentication system was designed, built, and deployed — including every problem encountered and how it was solved.

1

Overview

The authentication system is a site wide feature that powers all tools on this portfolio. It allows visitors to register for a free account, log in, and access AI tools with unlimited usage. Unregistered guests can try each tool up to 3 times before being prompted to create an account.

Rather than using a third party authentication service, this was built using Django's built in authentication framework — giving full control over the registration flow, session management, and usage tracking. User data is stored in a PostgreSQL database hosted on Neon and connected to the Django project via an environment variable.

The system was designed to be reusable across all five portfolio projects. Any new app simply checks whether the user is authenticated before granting access, without duplicating any authentication logic.

2

Tech Stack

Django Auth

Django's built in authentication system handling login, logout, registration, password hashing, and session management.

PostgreSQL

Industry standard relational database used to store user accounts, passwords, and usage tracking records in production.

Neon

Serverless PostgreSQL hosting platform built specifically for Vercel deployments. Provides a free tier generous enough for a portfolio project.

Django Sessions

Used to track guest usage across multiple page loads without requiring an account. Stores the usage count in a browser cookie.

psycopg2

Python driver that acts as the translator between Django and PostgreSQL. Required for Django to communicate with the Neon database.

dj-database-url

Converts the Neon connection string stored in the environment variable into the format Django expects for its database configuration.

3

Architecture

Authentication lives in its own dedicated Django app called accounts. This keeps all authentication logic, views, templates, and models in one self contained place that any other app can reference without duplicating code.

Folder Structure 

accounts/
  models.py          — UsageTracking model
  views.py           — register, login, logout, profile
  urls.py            — URL routing for auth pages
  templates/
    accounts/
      register.html  — registration form
      login.html     — login form
      profile.html   — user dashboard

Guest vs Registered User Flow 

Guest visits a tool
      ↓
Django checks session for usage count
      ↓
Under 3 uses → allow access, increment session count
Over 3 uses  → redirect to register page
      ↓
User registers → UsageTracking record created
      ↓
Registered user → unlimited access
Usage count stored in database, shown on profile

Database Models 

Django's built in User model:
  username, email, password (hashed), date_joined

Custom UsageTracking model:
  user          — OneToOneField to User
  summariser_uses — IntegerField (default 0)
  sentiment_uses  — IntegerField (default 0)
  created_at    — DateTimeField (auto)
  updated_at    — DateTimeField (auto)
4

Step by Step Build

01 Set Up Neon PostgreSQL Database

Created a free account on neon.tech and set up a new PostgreSQL project. The connection string was saved to the .env file locally and added to Vercel's environment variables for production. This connection string contains the database credentials and must never be committed to GitHub.

02 Connect Django to PostgreSQL

Installed psycopg2-binary and dj-database-url. Updated settings.py to replace the default SQLite configuration with a PostgreSQL connection driven by the DATABASE_URL environment variable: 

DATABASES = {
    'default': dj_database_url.config(
        default=os.environ.get('DATABASE_URL'),
        conn_max_age=600,
        ssl_require=True
    )
}

03 Create the Accounts App

Created a dedicated accounts app using python manage.py startapp accounts and registered it in settings.py. A custom UsageTracking model was created to store per user tool usage counts. This model has a OneToOneField relationship with Django's built in User model, meaning each user gets exactly one usage record.

04 Build Registration and Login Views

Wrote four views in accounts/views.py — register_view, login_view, logout_view, and profile_view. The registration view validates all input server side before creating the user, automatically creates a UsageTracking record, and logs the user in immediately after registration. The login view uses Django's built in authenticate() function which handles password verification securely.

05 Implement the 3 Free Uses System

For guest users, usage is tracked in Django's session system rather than the database since they do not have an account. Each tool view checks the session count before allowing access. After 3 uses, guests are redirected to the register page with an explanatory message. Registered users always have unlimited access and their usage is tracked in the database for display on their profile page. 

session_uses = request.session.get('summariser_uses', 0)
if session_uses >= FREE_USES:
    return redirect('/accounts/register')
request.session['summariser_uses'] = session_uses + 1

06 Add Auth Links to Navbar

Added conditional auth links to the navbar on all pages using Django's template language. When a user is logged in, their username and a sign out link are shown. When they are not logged in, sign in and register links are displayed instead. The same logic was applied to both the desktop and mobile navbar menus.

5

Key Concepts Learned

Django's Built In Authentication

Django ships with a complete authentication system including a User model, password hashing, login and logout functions, and session management. None of this needs to be built from scratch — it is configured and extended rather than written.

Password Hashing

Django never stores passwords as plain text. When a user registers, the password is passed through a one way hashing algorithm before being saved to the database. This means even if the database was compromised, the original passwords could not be recovered.

Database Migrations

Every time a model is created or changed, two commands are needed. makemigrations creates a migration file describing what changed. migrate applies that change to the actual database. Forgetting to run migrate after makemigrations is one of the most common Django mistakes.

OneToOneField Relationships

The UsageTracking model uses a OneToOneField to link to Django's User model. This enforces that each user gets exactly one usage record — never more. It is a way of extending the built in User model with custom data without modifying Django's core code.

Sessions vs Database Storage

Guest usage is tracked in Django's session system — temporary storage tied to a browser cookie. Registered user usage is tracked in the database permanently. This distinction is important: sessions are temporary and can be cleared, while database records persist indefinitely.

The @login_required Decorator

Adding @login_required above any view function instantly protects that page — unauthenticated users are automatically redirected to the login page. This is how Django protects pages that require authentication without writing any manual redirect logic.

Environment Variables for Database Credentials

The database connection string contains sensitive credentials and is stored in the .env file locally and in Vercel's environment variables in production. It is never committed to GitHub. This follows the same pattern established for the Django secret key and Anthropic API key.

6

Problems & Solutions

Every problem below was encountered during the real build of this feature.

Problem 1 — AttributeError before views were written

Error 

AttributeError: module 'accounts.views' has no attribute 'register_view'

Cause

The urls.py file was created and referenced view functions before those functions were written in views.py. Django tried to load the URLs on server start and could not find the functions.

Solution

Added placeholder view functions to views.py immediately after creating the URL file. This allowed the server to start while the real views were built one by one. Importantly the migrations had already completed successfully before this error appeared — the two processes are completely separate.

Problem 2 — UsageTracking table did not exist

Error 

ProgrammingError: relation "accounts_usagetracking" does not exist

Cause

The migration for the UsageTracking model had not been applied to the Neon database. The makemigrations command creates the migration file but migrate must also be run to apply it to the actual database.

Solution 

python manage.py makemigrations accounts
python manage.py migrate
Problem 3 — Tailwind CDN not rendering conditional banner classes

Problem

The guest usage banner was present in the HTML but completely invisible because it had no visible styling. The banner used Django template tags inside Tailwind class attributes to conditionally apply background and border colours.

Cause

The Tailwind CDN version only generates CSS for classes it can see as plain text in the HTML. Classes hidden inside Django template tags are invisible to the Tailwind scanner so no CSS is generated for them.

Solution

Replaced all conditional Tailwind classes with inline style attributes. Inline styles bypass Tailwind entirely and are applied directly by the browser regardless of what the CDN scanner can see.

Problem 4 — Debug print statement in wrong function

Problem

A debug print statement added to diagnose the banner issue never appeared in the terminal even when visiting the summariser page.

Cause

The print statement had accidentally been placed inside the documentation view function rather than the summariser view function. Since the documentation page was not being visited, the print statement never ran.

Solution

Moved the print statement to the correct function. This highlighted the importance of always checking which function your code is actually inside, especially in files with multiple functions.

Problem 5 — Django template filter breaking across multiple lines

Problem

The banner was showing the raw text "remaining" instead of the actual number. The pluralize filter was also displaying as literal text rather than being processed.

Cause

Django template tags and filters break when split across multiple lines in the HTML. The auto formatter in VS Code had wrapped the template tags across lines which caused Django to stop processing them correctly.

Solution

Moved all template tags onto a single line. Django template tags must never be broken across multiple lines — even if it makes the HTML harder to read.

7

Future Improvements

Email Verification

Send a verification email after registration to confirm the user owns the email address they provided. Django has built in support for this via its email framework.

Password Reset

Allow users to reset their password via email if they forget it. Django's built in password reset views make this straightforward to implement.

Usage History

Track individual tool uses with timestamps so users can see a full history of what they have summarised or analysed, not just a total count.

Harder Usage Limits

The current guest limit is session based and can be bypassed by clearing cookies. A more robust approach would tie limits to IP addresses combined with sessions, making it harder to circumvent without an account.

</>
AS.
